title: ‘Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse’ Is Here to Save Us
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins bring in Charles Holmes of The Midnight Boys to talk through Across the Spider-Verse: the animation, the multiversal canon plot, and that cliffhanger ending.

segments.heading: Across the Spider-Verse
segments.note: five stars, Sean's first in three years

films.title: Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse
films.note: Directed by Joachim Dos Santos, Kemp Powers, and Justin K. Thompson (three directors this time, versus Bob Persichetti/Peter Ramsey/Rodney Rothman on the first film), scripted by Lord & Miller with Dave Callahan. Every universe gets its own animation style: Gwen's world in watercolor/impressionist brushstrokes, a Bill Sienkiewicz-inspired look elsewhere. Charles reads it as a love letter to the non-house-style Marvel/DC artists. Jason Schwartzman voices the Spot, a D-list Marvel villain since the 1980s; Oscar Isaac plays Miguel O'Hara/Spider-Man 2099, enforcing "canon events" against Miles; Hailee Steinfeld's Gwen Stacy gets far more space than in the first film. Runs 2 hours 20 minutes and ends on a hard cliffhanger (originally titled Part One) ahead of Beyond the Spider-Verse, dated for March 2024. Charles's one real reservation: unlike the tightly self-contained first film, this one starts to feel like "the machine," leaning on legacy-actor cameos (Andrew Garfield, Tobey Maguire, Donald Glover's Aaron Davis) the way No Way Home did.

films.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
films.note: The 2018 predecessor, a movie Sean calls a masterpiece and once swapped to Amanda. Its tight, self-contained structure (beginning, middle, end) is the yardstick Charles uses for his one complaint about the sequel's cliffhanger.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: The film Sean says "walked so this movie could run," training audiences to accept multiple Spider-Men onscreen at once. He liked it; the legacy-cameo playbook here reminds Charles of it a little too much.

films.title: Avatar: The Way of Water
films.note: Charles's touchstone for a movie that makes you wait years to show what it built, though he thinks it leans on archetypes too heavily where Across the Spider-Verse uses them as "a trampoline."

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: Charles's other reference for a film that announces the scope of its craft in its first five minutes.

films.title: Tár
films.note: Amanda's prior benchmark for five-star filmmaking this year; Sean argues the two movies have real overlap.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: Grouped with Tár as one of the small handful of recent films the hosts have given five stars.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: Cited for a chaotic final 45 minutes. The live-action MCU color palette, Amanda says, looks flat next to this movie's animation.

films.title: Ant-Man and the Wasp: Quantumania
films.note: A passing reference alongside the MCU's live-action house style.

films.title: Dune
films.note: The inverse cliffhanger experience: Sean didn't know going in that Dune was only Part One, whereas he knew this film was building toward Beyond the Spider-Verse.

films.title: Titan A.E.
films.note: Charles's example of the mid-budget animated movie pitched at 12- and 13-year-olds, a lane he says has largely disappeared.

films.title: Shrek
films.note: Another example of that same vanished for-everyone animated lane.
